title: SAA-C03 Daily Mock
subtitle: Day 2 — Access Keys & CLI, Public vs Private Services, Global Infrastructure, Default VPC, EC2 Basics
topics_covered: IAM Access Keys & AWS CLI v2
AWS Global Infrastructure (Regions, AZs, resilience tiers)
Review: IAM Basics (identities, authentication vs authorization, implicit deny)

questions.stem: A developer needs to interact with AWS services from the AWS CLI and from a script, rather than through the web console. Which credential type is designed for this kind of programmatic access by an IAM user?

options.text: Access keys — an access key ID together with a secret access key.
options.why: Correct — access keys are the long-term credential used for programmatic, CLI, and API access by an IAM user, separate from the console password.

options.text: The IAM user's console password and MFA code.
options.why: Wrong — a password (plus MFA) is for interactive console sign-in, not for programmatic API/CLI calls.

options.text: An SSH key pair downloaded from IAM.
options.why: Wrong — SSH key pairs are used to connect into instances, not to authenticate IAM API/CLI requests.

options.text: The account root user's email address and password.
options.why: Wrong — root credentials should never be used for routine programmatic access, and they are not the IAM-user mechanism described.

questions.stem: A security review examines how IAM access keys behave. Which TWO statements about IAM access keys are correct? (Select TWO.)

options.text: The secret access key is shown only once, at creation time, and cannot be retrieved again afterwards.
options.why: Correct — you must store the secret when it is created; AWS will only ever show the access key ID again, never the secret.

options.text: An IAM user can have up to two sets of access keys at a time, which supports rotation.
options.why: Correct — the two-key maximum lets you create a new key, switch over, and delete the old one without downtime.

options.text: Access keys are short-term credentials that expire automatically after a few hours.
options.why: Wrong — access keys are long-term credentials valid until deactivated or deleted; temporary, expiring credentials come from roles/STS.

options.text: Access keys can be attached to an IAM group so that all members share them.
options.why: Wrong — access keys belong to an individual IAM user; groups have no credentials of their own.

options.text: Deactivating an access key permanently deletes it and frees the slot.
options.why: Wrong — deactivating only makes the key inactive; it still occupies a slot until you actually delete it.

questions.stem: After creating access keys for an IAM user, an engineer wants to run AWS commands from their terminal using the AWS CLI v2. What is the standard way to make the CLI use those credentials?

options.text: Run `aws configure` and supply the access key ID, secret access key, default region, and output format.
options.why: Correct — `aws configure` stores the credentials (and region/output) in a profile that the CLI then uses for requests.

options.text: Paste the secret access key as an argument on every command.
options.why: Wrong — credentials are configured once, not passed per command; doing so would also repeatedly expose the secret.

options.text: Sign in to the web console first; the CLI reads that browser session.
options.why: Wrong — the CLI does not share the browser's console session; it needs its own configured credentials.

options.text: Enter the root user email and password when the CLI prompts.
options.why: Wrong — the CLI authenticates with IAM access keys, and the root user should not be used for CLI access.

questions.stem: An architect is explaining AWS connectivity models. A public AWS service (such as S3) is reached over public endpoints, while another category of service runs inside a customer's VPC. Which statement BEST captures the distinction?

options.text: Public services sit in the AWS public zone and are reached via public endpoints, but access is still controlled by permissions; private services run inside a VPC's private address space.
options.why: Correct — 'public' refers to network location and endpoint reachability, not open access — IAM permissions still apply, while private services live within a VPC.

options.text: Public services are open to anyone with no authentication, whereas private services require a password.
options.why: Wrong — being a public service does not mean unauthenticated access; permissions still gate who can use it.

options.text: Public services run inside your VPC and private services run on the public internet.
options.why: Wrong — this reverses the two concepts; private services are the ones inside the VPC.

options.text: Public versus private only indicates whether a service is free or paid.
options.why: Wrong — the terms describe network location/endpoints, not pricing.

questions.stem: When designing for resilience, an architect needs to understand AWS geography. Which statement correctly describes the relationship between an AWS Region and an Availability Zone (AZ)?

options.text: A Region is a geographically separate area that contains multiple, isolated Availability Zones.
options.why: Correct — each Region is independent and is made up of multiple AZs that are isolated from one another to contain faults.

options.text: An Availability Zone contains several independent Regions.
options.why: Wrong — the hierarchy is reversed; AZs sit inside a Region.

options.text: A Region and an Availability Zone are two names for the same thing.
options.why: Wrong — they are distinct; a Region groups multiple AZs.

options.text: Availability Zones in different Regions share the same physical hardware.
options.why: Wrong — Regions and their AZs are isolated and do not share hardware.

questions.stem: A workload component runs from a single Availability Zone, and if that one AZ fully fails the component goes offline. How is this component's resilience BEST classified?

options.text: AZ resilient — it can tolerate issues within the AZ but not the loss of the entire AZ.
options.why: Correct — a component confined to one AZ is AZ-resilient and fails if that AZ fails.

options.text: Region resilient — it survives the failure of an entire AZ.
options.why: Wrong — region-resilient implies spanning multiple AZs in a Region, which this single-AZ component does not.

options.text: Globally resilient — it survives the failure of an entire Region.
options.why: Wrong — that is the highest tier and clearly does not apply to something running in one AZ.

options.text: Edge resilient — it runs from CloudFront edge locations.
options.why: Wrong — this is not a real resilience tier for the component, and edge locations are unrelated to how it is deployed.

questions.stem: An engineer inspects the default VPC that AWS provides. Which TWO statements about the default VPC are correct? (Select TWO.)

options.text: There is at most one default VPC per Region, and it can be deleted and later recreated.
options.why: Correct — each Region has a single default VPC, and if it is deleted it can be regenerated from the console.

options.text: It is preconfigured for connectivity, with an internet gateway attached and a default subnet in each AZ.
options.why: Correct — the default VPC comes ready for public connectivity, including an IGW and a default subnet in each Availability Zone.

options.text: The default VPC spans every Region in the account at the same time.
options.why: Wrong — VPCs are regional; a default VPC exists within a single Region.

options.text: Resources in the default VPC cannot reach the internet without extra setup.
options.why: Wrong — the default VPC is already configured for internet connectivity out of the box.

options.text: AWS recommends the default VPC for all production workloads.
options.why: Wrong — custom VPCs are generally preferred for production; the default VPC is mainly a convenience.

questions.stem: An EC2 instance is launched into a subnet. From a connectivity and resilience standpoint, which statement BEST describes EC2 as a service?

options.text: EC2 is a private service: an instance runs in a subnet within a single Availability Zone, so it is AZ-resilient and fails if that AZ fails.
options.why: Correct — instances are private (VPC-based), tied to one subnet/AZ, and depend on that AZ's availability.

options.text: EC2 is a public service reachable directly from the internet without a VPC.
options.why: Wrong — EC2 is a private service that runs inside a VPC subnet.

options.text: A single EC2 instance automatically spans multiple Availability Zones for high availability.
options.why: Wrong — one instance lives in a single AZ; spanning AZs requires multiple instances and additional architecture.

options.text: EC2 instances are globally resilient and survive the loss of an entire Region.
options.why: Wrong — an instance is tied to one AZ in one Region, so it is not globally resilient.

questions.stem: A team is launching EC2 instances and deciding how they are created and sized. Which pairing of EC2 concepts is correct?

options.text: An AMI is the image used to launch an instance, while the instance type/size determines its CPU, memory, and other resources.
options.why: Correct — the AMI provides the boot template (OS/configuration) and the instance type defines the compute resources.

options.text: The AMI determines CPU and memory, while the instance type supplies the operating system image.
options.why: Wrong — these two roles are reversed.

options.text: The instance type both boots the operating system and stores all data permanently.
options.why: Wrong — the instance type defines resources; the AMI provides the OS, and storage comes from EBS or instance store.

options.text: An AMI can be used only once and is deleted after a single launch.
options.why: Wrong — an AMI can launch many instances and is not consumed when used.

questions.stem: To cut costs overnight, an engineer weighs whether to stop or terminate development EC2 instances that use EBS volumes. Which statement is correct?

options.text: Stopping an instance keeps its EBS volume and data so it can be started again later; compute charges pause while EBS storage charges continue.
options.why: Correct — a stopped instance retains its EBS data and can be restarted; you stop paying for compute but keep paying for the EBS storage.

options.text: Stopping and terminating an instance have the same effect on its data.
options.why: Wrong — terminating (by default) deletes the instance and its root EBS volume, whereas stopping preserves them.

options.text: A stopped instance keeps incurring full compute charges as if it were running.
options.why: Wrong — you are not billed for the compute of a stopped instance, though storage charges still apply.

options.text: Terminating an instance lets you start the same instance again later.
options.why: Wrong — termination is permanent; that instance cannot be restarted.

questions.stem: A company maps out which IAM identity to use in different situations. Which mapping is correct?

options.text: Use an IAM user for a specific person or application with long-term credentials, an IAM role for something that needs temporary assumed credentials, and an IAM group to organize users for permission management.
options.why: Correct — users are long-term identities, roles provide temporary assumed access, and groups are management containers for users.

options.text: Use an IAM group to sign in directly, and an IAM role only for human console access.
options.why: Wrong — groups cannot be signed into, and roles are about temporary assumed access rather than a human console identity.

options.text: Use the root user as the standard identity for applications.
options.why: Wrong — applications should use roles (or users), never the root user.

options.text: Use an IAM role to hold permanent credentials embedded in application code.
options.why: Wrong — roles exist to provide temporary credentials precisely so that long-term keys are not embedded in code.

questions.stem: An IAM user signs in with valid credentials, then attempts to delete a resource, but the action is denied because no policy permits it. The successful sign-in and the separate permission check are examples of which two steps, in order?

options.text: Authentication first (proving identity), then authorization (checking what that identity may do).
options.why: Correct — the identity is verified first, and only then are its permissions evaluated for the requested action.

options.text: Authorization first, then authentication.
options.why: Wrong — permissions can only be evaluated after the identity is established, so this order is reversed.

options.text: Both steps are authentication; authorization is not involved.
options.why: Wrong — the policy/permission check is authorization, a distinct step from verifying identity.

options.text: Both steps are authorization; authentication is not involved.
options.why: Wrong — verifying the sign-in credentials is authentication, which is separate from the permission check.

questions.stem: A brand-new IAM user is created with no policies attached and immediately tries to list resources in the account. What happens, and why?

options.text: The request is denied, because a new identity has no permissions by default and access must be explicitly granted (implicit deny).
options.why: Correct — IAM denies by default; with no explicit allow, a new user can perform no actions.

options.text: The request succeeds, because new IAM users automatically receive read access.
options.why: Wrong — there is no automatic access; new users start with no permissions at all.

options.text: The request succeeds, because IAM allows everything unless something is explicitly denied.
options.why: Wrong — IAM's default posture is implicit deny, not implicit allow.

options.text: The request is denied only if an explicit deny policy is attached.
options.why: Wrong — even with no policy at all, the default implicit deny blocks the action.
